backgroundImageAltText: Medical education diagram with interactive hotspots

params.text: The subclavian artery becomes the axillary artery at the outer border of the first rib. It is the sole arterial supply to the upper limb. Clinical pearl: Subclavian artery compression against the first rib is tested by Adson's test in thoracic outlet syndrome.

Pectoralis minor divides the axillary artery into three parts: 1st part (medial), 2nd part (behind), and 3rd part (lateral). Memory aid: The number of branches follows the 1-2-3 rule — 1 branch from part 1, 2 from part 2, and 3 from part 3.

The thoracoacromial artery is a short trunk from the 2nd part of the axillary artery. It pierces the clavipectoral fascia and divides into four branches: Pectoral, Acromial, Clavicular, and Deltoid. Memory aid: "PACD" for the four branches.

The subscapular artery is the largest branch of the axillary artery, arising from the 3rd part. It divides into the circumflex scapular and thoracodorsal arteries. The circumflex scapular artery passes through the triangular space.

The axillary artery becomes the brachial artery at the lower border of teres major. It is the site for routine blood pressure measurement. Clinical pearl: The brachial artery pulse is palpated medial to the biceps tendon in the cubital fossa.
